flags.note: אֱלֹהִים — Grammatically plural, normally takes singular verbs when referring to Israel's God. Translating as "God" (singular) interprets the plural; "gods" would be technically accurate but theologically wrong in monotheistic contexts.
יהוה — The divine name (YHWH). LORD (small caps) hides the personal name. Yahweh/Jehovah are reconstructions of the consonants. The MKT's choice shapes the reader's sense of intimacy with God.

to cut (off, down or asunder); by implication, to destroy or consume; specifically, to covenant (i.e. make an alliance or bargain, originally by cutting flesh and passing between the pieces)
the face (as the part that turns); used in a great variety of applications (literally and figuratively); also (with prepositional prefix) as a preposition (before, etc.)

flags.note: בְּרִית — "covenant." The full weight: a formal, oath-bound relationship with stipulations and consequences. English "covenant" is thinner than the Hebrew concept.
יהוה — The divine name (YHWH). LORD (small caps) hides the personal name. Yahweh/Jehovah are reconstructions of the consonants. The MKT's choice shapes the reader's sense of intimacy with God.

Ziph, the name of a place in Palestine; also of an Israelite
a forest (perhaps as furnishing the material for fabric)
to go (causatively, bring) out, in a great variety of applications, literally and figuratively, direct and proxim.
a pasture (i.e. open field, whither cattle are driven); by implication, a desert; also speech (including its organs)
properly, a breathing creature, i.e. animal of (abstractly) vitality; used very widely in a literal, accommodated or figurative sense (bodily or mental)
to see, literally or figuratively (in numerous applications, direct and implied, transitive, intransitive and causative)
Shaul, the name of an Edomite and two Israelites

flags.note: נֶפֶשׁ — "soul" (Greek-influenced: non-material self) vs. "life" (the whole living person) vs. "self" (the person as subject). Hebrew nefesh is embodied, not a separate immaterial substance.
